MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
Off Calendar May-25-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Allen Amarkarian, Esq. 310-933-4271 Quill & Arrow for Plaintiff.
Oliver Smith, Esq. 415-362-2580 Lewis Brisbois Bisgaard & Smith LLP for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. Mr. Smith argues equitable estoppel. Mr. Amarkarian responds. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT NISSAN NORTH AMERICA, INC. MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant NISSAN NORTH AMERICA, INC.'s motion to compel arbitration and stay is denied.

The court rejects nonsignatory defendant's argument that it can compel arbitration based on equitable estoppel. The equitable estoppel rule holds that a party invoking one part of a contract cannot object to the other side invoking some other part. In the arbitration context, the "application of equitable estoppel as the basis for allowing a nonsignatory to enforce an arbitration clause is that the claims the plaintiff asserts against the nonsignatory must be dependent upon, or founded in and inextricably intertwined with, the underlying contractual obligations of the agreement containing the arbitration clause." (Goldman v. KPMG, LLP (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 209, 217-218.) Equitable estoppel precludes a party from claiming the benefits of a contract while simultaneously attempting to avoid the burdens that contract imposes. (Philadelphia Indemnity Ins. Co. v. SMG Holdings, Inc. (2019) 44 Cal.App.5th 834, 841.)

The central issue is whether the plaintiffs' claims are "intimately founded in and intertwined' with the underlying contract obligations." Martha Ochoa v. Ford Motor Company (Ford Motor Warranty Cases) (2023) 89 Cal.App.5th 1324, __, 306 Cal.Rptr.3d 611, 618, quoting (and then disagreeing with) Felisilda v. FCA US LLC (2020) 53 Cal.App.5th 486. At first blush the claims seem intertwined: After all, plaintiff needed to enter into the Retail Installment Contract - Simple Finance Charge (With Arbitration Provision) ("RISC") to purchase the vehicle and obtain the warranties. Nevertheless, closer review demonstrates that plaintiff's action is not in fact intertwined with the RISC. Plaintiff is solely suing for violations of the Song-Beverly Act and as Ochoa explains, the cited warranties are independent from the sale agreement.

The court notes Felisdilda is distinguishable. There, the plaintiff asserted a single cause of action against the manufacturer and dealership and the dealership sought arbitration. In "Felisilda, it was the dealership-a signatory to the purchase agreement-that moved to compel arbitration rather than the non-signatory manufacturer." (Ngo v. BMW of North America, LLC (9th Cir. 2022) 23 F.4th 942, 950; see also e.g., Glassburg v. Ford Motor Company (C.D. Cal., Nov. 2, 2021, No. 221CV01333ODWMAAX) 2021 WL 5086358, at *3; In re ZF-TRW Airbag Control Units Products Liability Litigation (C.D. Cal., Sept. 27, 2021, No. LAML1902905JAKFFMX) 2021 WL 5069003, at *5 & n.1.) In this case, plaintiff is only suing the manufacturer for statutory violations. "Plaintiff is seeking to recover for Defendant's alleged breaches of its manufacturer warranties, which arose independently of the Lease Agreement." (Tawil v. BMW of North America, LLC (C.D. Cal., May 12, 2022, No. CV2109257RSWLJPRX) 2022 WL 19076675, at *3.) In other words, the fact that plaintiffs invoke warranties does not mean they invoke the RISC.

Mr. Amarkarian to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)
